questions.question: Explain the four phases of the instruction cycle with a diagram representation.
keyPoints: Fetch Phase: PC → MAR → Memory → MBR → IR, PC+1 → PC
Decode Phase: Instruction in IR is interpreted, control signals generated
Execute Phase: ALU performs operation, data transfer occurs
Store Phase: Result stored back to memory or register

questions.question: Differentiate between Zero-address, One-address, Two-address, and Three-address instruction formats.
keyPoints: Zero-address: Stack operations, operand on stack (e.g., PUSH, POP)
One-address: Accumulator architecture, one operand implicit (ACC)
Two-address: Two operand addresses, one serves as destination
Three-address: Two source + one destination address, most flexible

questions.question: What is the role of Control Unit in instruction execution?
keyPoints: Generates timing and control signals
Coordinates between CPU components (ALU, registers, memory)
Controls data flow during fetch-decode-execute cycle
Interprets opcode and determines required microoperations

questions.question: Define and differentiate between: (i) Direct and Indirect addressing (ii) Immediate and Register addressing.
keyPoints: Direct: Address field contains actual operand address in memory
Indirect: Address field contains pointer/address of operand's address
Immediate: Operand value is given directly in instruction
Register: Operand is located in a CPU register

questions.question: Draw and explain the data flow during the Fetch Cycle.
keyPoints: PC → MAR: Transfer address of next instruction
Memory Read: Fetch instruction from memory address in MAR
MBR → IR: Load fetched instruction into Instruction Register
PC + 1 → PC: Increment program counter

questions.question: Explain the concept of microoperations. Give two examples of microoperations in fetch cycle.
keyPoints: Microoperations are elementary operations performed by CPU
Examples: PC → MAR (transfer address), MAR → Memory (address bus)
MBR → IR (load instruction), PC + 1 → PC (increment)

questions.question: What is the significance of opcode in an instruction? How does it determine the operation to be performed?
keyPoints: Opcode specifies the operation type (ADD, SUB, LOAD, STORE, etc.)
CPU decoder interprets opcode to generate appropriate control signals
Different opcodes cause different microoperations in execute phase
Determines whether ALU operation, data transfer, or control jump occurs

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the complete instruction cycle. Include all phases and show how data flows between CPU registers and memory.
keyPoints: Draw fetch cycle: PC→MAR→Memory→MBR→IR→PC+1
Decode phase: IR→Control Unit, generate control signals
Execute phase: Depends on opcode - ALU ops/data transfer/branch
Store phase: Write result back to memory/register
Explain each register's role: PC, MAR, MBR, IR, ACC, ALU
Mention timing and control signal generation

questions.question: Explain different instruction formats used in computer architecture. Compare their advantages and disadvantages with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Zero-address (Stack): Advantages - simple, less bits; Disadvantage - limited operations
One-address (Accumulator): Advantages - one address field; Disadvantage - accumulator bottleneck
Two-address: Advantages - flexible, common in practice; Disadvantage - one operand overwritten
Three-address: Advantages - most flexible, readable; Disadvantage - requires more bits, longer instructions

questions.question: Explain various addressing modes with examples. How do they affect instruction execution and memory access?
keyPoints: Immediate: Operand in instruction (e.g., ADD #5) - no memory access
Direct: Address in instruction points to operand (e.g., ADD 100) - one memory access
Indirect: Address field has pointer to operand (e.g., ADD @100) - two memory accesses
Register: Operand in register (e.g., ADD R1) - no memory access
Indexed: Address + index register - useful for arrays
Relative: Address relative to PC - used in branches/jumps

questions.question: Describe the internal organization of CPU with a block diagram. Explain how registers cooperate during instruction execution.
keyPoints: Block diagram showing: Control Unit, ALU, Register Set, Buses
Explain each component: CU (control signals), ALU (operations), Registers (storage)
CPU registers: PC, IR, MAR, MBR, ACC, General purpose registers
Data flow through buses between components
Timing and synchronization of operations
